name: Francis X. Clooney
dates_notes: Parkman Professor of Divinity at Harvard Divinity School; Jesuit theologian; Director of the Center for the Study of World Religions 2010–2017.

dates_evidence.description: Public biographical records: born 1950, ordained Jesuit priest, Harvard Divinity School appointment from 2005, Center for the Study of World Religions directorship 2010–2017, elected to the American Academy of Arts and Sciences 2025.

core_thesis: Francis X. Clooney's project is comparative theology in the technical sense he himself fixed: a discipline that engages the primary texts of one tradition from within the formed disciplinary commitments of another, neither collapsing the two into religious-studies neutrality nor treating one as propaedeutic to the other. The Indic side is sustained Sanskrit and Tamil work on Rāmānuja's *Śrī-Bhāṣya*, on the Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra-Bhāṣya tradition, on Vedānta-Deśika's *Rahasya-Traya-Sāra*, and on the Tamil *Divya Prabandham* as the Tenkalai *vyākhyāna*-tradition received it. Three contributions structure the corpus. *Thinking Ritually* (1990) reads Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā as a coherent ritual system in which the agent's intentionality, qualification, and reflective stance are constitutive of the ritual operation. *Theology After Vedānta* (1993) treats the *Śrī-Bhāṣya* as the methodological model for what theology as sustained primary-text reading can be — its *Mahā-Pūrvapakṣa* / *Mahā-Siddhānta* structure on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.1](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.1.1) and its *sapta-vidhānupapatti* against *avidyā* are read as constructive theological argument carried out in commentarial form. *Beyond Compare* (2008) reads Vedānta-Deśika's *Rahasya-Traya-Sāra* alongside Francis de Sales' *Treatise on the Love of God*, working through Deśika's six *aṅga*-s of *prapatti* (*ānukūlya-saṅkalpa*, *prātikūlya-varjana*, *rakṣiṣyatīti viśvāsa*, *goptṛtva-varaṇa*, *ātma-nikṣepa*, *kārpaṇya*) on the Vaḍakalai *markaṭa-nyāya* construal of grace as cooperating with the agent's continuing co-action. The 2022 encyclopedia entry "Mīmāṃsā as Introspective Literature and as Philosophy" extends the 1990 reading via Kumārila's *Ślokavārttika* and Śabara on [*Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra* 1.1.2](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.2): Mīmāṃsā thematises the practitioner's interior orientation to the sacred, not only the philosophy of language. Across the corpus Clooney is the principal English-language reader of Viśiṣṭādvaita and Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā as living theological-philosophical traditions whose internal coherence is the precondition for responsible comparison. Honest scope: Clooney's monographs are not held in this corpus on disk; the engagement here proceeds via published reviews (van den Bosch, Verpoorten, Scharfe, Schmalz, Kunjunni Raja on *Thinking Ritually*) and the published bibliographic and abstract record of each title (Vienna 1990 record; SUNY 1993 record; Springer 2022 record), together with the standing scholarly attribution of the doctrinal positions summarized above.

engaged_works.title: Thinking Ritually: Rediscovering the Purva Mimamsa of Jaimini
engaged_works.title_iast: Thinking Ritually: Rediscovering the Pūrva Mīmāṃsā of Jaimini
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Published 1990 in the Publications of the De Nobili Research Library, vol. XVII, Vienna; circulated by Brill outside Vienna.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored monograph (Publications of the De Nobili Research Library XVII, Vienna 1990).

ascription_evidence.description: Standard reference work in English-language Mīmāṃsā scholarship since publication.

engaged_works.summary: A monograph-length re-reading of Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā centered on Jaimini's *Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra* and Śabara's *Bhāṣya*, with sustained engagement of Kumārila's *Tantra-Vārttika* and Prabhākara's *Bṛhatī*. The decisive move is methodological: rather than treating Mīmāṃsā as a curiosity in the history of Indian philosophy of language, Clooney reads it as a coherent ritual *system* whose technical apparatus — *codanā* as autonomous *pramāṇa*, the *bhāvanā* analysis of injunctive force, the *aṅga-aṅgi-vibhāga*, the *triputī-saṃvit* / *jñātatā* dispute on cognition's self-disclosure, the *adhikāra* doctrine of intrinsic ritual qualification — articulates the agent's intentionality and reflective stance as internal to the ritual operation, not external prerequisites. The book is the first sustained English-language argument that Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā is *thinking* in a strong sense — the ritualist is constitutively a reflective subject — and it remains the standard English entry-point to the Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra-Bhāṣya for graduate work. The reading anchors Clooney's later 2022 encyclopedia identification of Mīmāṃsā as introspective literature and supplies the framework in which Vedāntic engagements with the *karma-kāṇḍa* (Bādarāyaṇa, Śaṅkara at *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.1.4, Maṇḍana on *bhāvanā* and *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*) become readable as engagements with a system that is itself doing more than ritual procedure.

engaged_works.title: Theology After Vedanta: An Experiment in Comparative Theology
engaged_works.title_iast: Theology After Vedānta: An Experiment in Comparative Theology
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Published 1993, State University of New York Press (SUNY Series, Toward a Comparative Philosophy of Religions).

ascription_evidence.description: Programmatic statement of comparative theology cited across the field.

engaged_works.summary: The programmatic statement of comparative theology as Clooney conceives it, grounded in sustained reading of Rāmānuja's *Śrī-Bhāṣya*. The argument has two halves. First, the *Śrī-Bhāṣya* is treated as a living methodological model rather than as a museum-object: its *Mahā-Pūrvapakṣa* / *Mahā-Siddhānta* structure on [*Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.1](cite://badarayana/brahma-sutra/1.1.1), its sustained *sāmānādhikaraṇya*-analysis of *tat tvam asi*, and its *sapta-vidhānupapatti* against *avidyā* are read as the work of a theologian for whom commentary on a received *sūtra*-corpus is the constitutive form of constructive theological argument. Second, this is offered as the model for what Catholic theology *after* — that is, having read — Vedānta might be: not theology with Vedānta as an external object, but theology whose constructive moves take place in the disciplined company of Vedāntic primary texts, with the reading itself constitutive of the theological position rather than preparatory to it. The book is the principal English-language sustained engagement with Rāmānuja's *Śrī-Bhāṣya* methodology and is the standard reference for Rāmānuja in late-twentieth-century theology of religions. The book reads the *Śrī-Bhāṣya* on its own terms as a constructive theological argument rather than as a data-point in the history of ideas, and is the principal English-language sustained engagement with Rāmānuja's commentarial methodology.

engaged_works.title: Seeing Through Texts: Doing Theology among the Srivaisnavas of South India
engaged_works.title_iast: Seeing Through Texts: Doing Theology among the Śrīvaiṣṇavas of South India
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Published 1996, State University of New York Press.

ascription_evidence.description: Standard English-language reference for the Tenkalai Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa commentarial tradition on the Divya Prabandham.

engaged_works.summary: An extended reading of the Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa Śrīvaiṣṇava commentarial tradition on Nāmmāḻvār's *Tiruvāymoḻi* and the *Divya Prabandham* — Tirukkurukai-pirān-piḷḷān's *Āṛāyirappaḍi*, Naṅjīyar's *Onpadināyirappaḍi*, Periyavāccān-piḷḷai's *Irupattu-nālāyirappaḍi*, Vaḍakku-tiru-vīdhi-piḷḷai's *Muppattāṛāyirappaḍi*, and Maṇavāḷa Māmunigaḷ's culminating *vyākhyāna* — and the theological work the *vyākhyāna*-tradition does. Clooney's contention is that the Tenkalai *vyākhyāna* is not auxiliary devotional commentary but the constitutive theological literature of the school: the doctrinal positions of *prapatti*-soteriology, *Drāviḍa-Veda* status of the Tamil hymns, *ācārya-abhimāna*, and *bhāgavata-śeṣatva* are *carried* by the act of reading the *prabandham* through these *vyākhyāna*-s, not by separate doctrinal treatises. Nāthamuni's recovery of the Āḻvārs' hymns and his integration of Pāñcarātra liturgical theology with Vedāntic *upāsanā* are treated as the originating act of this commentarial culture. The book is the principal English-language engagement with Tenkalai theology as a *vyākhyāna*-tradition and supplies the methodological frame in which Pillai-Lokācārya's *Śrī-Vacana-Bhūṣaṇa* and Māmunigaḷ's commentary on it are read as the school's primary theological literature.

engaged_works.title: Hindu God, Christian God: How Reason Helps Break Down the Boundaries between Religions

ascription_evidence.description: Standard reference in English-language theology of religions.

engaged_works.summary: A comparative-theological monograph organized around four classical theological loci — the existence of God, divine embodiment, divine self-revelation, and the singularity of the savior — argued by paired reading of Vedāntic theism (chiefly Vedānta-Deśika and the Viśiṣṭādvaita tradition, with selective engagement of Madhva and the Pāñcarātra defense-tradition) against canonical Catholic and Protestant theology. The methodological wager is that *reasoned theological argument* — not phenomenology of religion, not history of religion — is the discipline within which interreligious comparison is best conducted; the *Śatadūṣaṇī*'s argument-by-argument structure and the Catholic *quaestio disputata* form are read as kindred modes of theological reasoning. The book treats Viśiṣṭādvaita theism as a genuine philosophical-theological position — *svatantra* Brahman with *cit* and *acit* as *apṛthak-siddha* body, real divine attributes (*ananta-kalyāṇa-guṇa*), real *avatāra* — and not as a sectarian variant whose claims are pre-emptively softened by being labeled "religious experience." The book establishes Clooney's position that the Vedāntic schools are dialogue partners at the level of theological reasoning proper.

engaged_works.title: Beyond Compare: St Francis de Sales and Sri Vedanta Desika on Loving Surrender to God
engaged_works.title_iast: Beyond Compare: St. Francis de Sales and Śrī Vedānta Deśika on Loving Surrender to God

ascription_evidence.description: Standard English-language reference for Vedānta-Deśika's Rahasya-Traya-Sāra in comparative theology.

engaged_works.summary: A paired reading of Vedānta-Deśika's *Rahasya-Traya-Sāra* — the canonical Vaḍakalai Tamil-Maṇipravāḷa rahasya in 32 *adhikāra*-s on the three Śrīvaiṣṇava esoteric *mantra*-s — and Francis de Sales' *Treatise on the Love of God* (1616), the major Salesian devotional theology. The substantive thesis is that the two texts converge on a structurally analogous account of *prapatti* / loving surrender as a soteriologically primary act, and divide on whether grace operates by cooperating with the agent's continuing co-action (Deśika's *markaṭa-nyāya*; de Sales' broadly Tridentine cooperation-of-the-will position) or in some stronger sense without that co-action. Clooney works through Deśika's six *aṅga*-s of *prapatti* — *ānukūlya-saṅkalpa*, *prātikūlya-varjana*, *rakṣiṣyatīti viśvāsa*, *goptṛtva-varaṇa*, *ātma-nikṣepa*, *kārpaṇya* — as conditions the surrendered self enacts in cooperation with grace, against the contrastive Tenkalai construal, and reads them alongside de Sales' phenomenology of the loving will. The book is the principal sustained English-language engagement with Deśika's *Rahasya-Traya-Sāra* in any language and is the standard reference for the Vaḍakalai-Tenkalai contrast in comparative theology.

engaged_works.title: Comparative Theology: Deep Learning Across Religious Borders
engaged_works.title_iast: Comparative Theology: Deep Learning Across Religious Borders

ascription_evidence.description: The standard methodological statement of comparative theology in English.

engaged_works.summary: Clooney's mature methodological statement of comparative theology as a discipline. The book distinguishes the discipline from the older theology of religions (which classifies traditions by their relation to Christian salvation), from religious-studies comparison (which prescinds from theological commitment), and from interreligious dialogue (which is institutional rather than textual), and argues that comparative theology is constitutively the slow, patient reading of primary texts of another tradition by a theologian who reads from within their own. The methodological norms are made explicit: long apprenticeship in the languages and the *vyākhyāna*-traditions of the other tradition; refusal of premature synthesis; willingness to be changed by what one reads; the discipline of letting the comparison surface from the texts rather than imposing a comparative grid. The book codifies the methodological standards under which Clooney's engagements with Rāmānuja, Vedānta-Deśika, Pillai-Lokācārya, and the Mīmāṃsakas operate, making those engagements citable as the work of a discipline rather than as one scholar's idiosyncratic reading.

engaged_works.title: His Hiding Place is Darkness: A Hindu-Catholic Theopoetics of Divine Absence
engaged_works.title_iast: His Hiding Place is Darkness: A Hindu-Catholic Theopoetics of Divine Absence

ascription_evidence.description: Comparative-theological monograph in the Stanford Cultural Memory in the Present series.

engaged_works.summary: A paired reading of the *viraha* (separation) literature of Nāmmāḻvār's *Tiruvāymoḻi* — the Āḻvār's longing for the absent Lord, read through the Tenkalai *vyākhyāna*-tradition — and the *cantique-spirituel* tradition of John of the Cross, on the experience of divine absence as constitutively part of the soul's relation to God. The book extends the methodological commitments of *Beyond Compare* into the more difficult territory of mystical literature, where the comparison must work through the interior phenomenology of *bhakti* and *amor* rather than through doctrinal propositions. The book gives the most sustained English-language engagement with the *viraha-bhakti* register of *Tiruvāymoḻi* — the register the Tenkalai *vyākhyāna*-tradition reads as the lived enactment of *prapatti* — and supplies the affective-phenomenological complement to *Seeing Through Texts*' more architectural engagement with the same commentarial corpus.

engaged_works.title: Reading the Hindu and Christian Classics: Why and How Deep Learning Still Matters
engaged_works.title_iast: Reading the Hindu and Christian Classics: Why and How Deep Learning Still Matters
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Published 2019, University of Virginia Press (Richard Lectures).
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored monograph (University of Virginia Press 2019; based on the Richard Lectures).

ascription_evidence.description: Lecture-based monograph on the discipline of reading classical religious texts.

engaged_works.summary: A short methodological-pedagogical statement, based on the Richard Lectures at the University of Virginia, on what the slow disciplined reading of classical religious texts — *Tiruvāymoḻi* and the Pauline epistles, the *Bhagavad-Gītā* and the *Spiritual Exercises* of Ignatius — supplies that more abstract theological synthesis cannot. The argument completes the sequence begun in *Theology After Vedānta* and codified in *Comparative Theology* (2010): the constitutive act of theological reasoning across traditions is the long apprenticeship in primary texts, and the practical question of *how* to read those texts — what the *vyākhyāna*-tradition of the host tradition has already done with them, what the slow attention to a single passage discloses that the survey-treatment occludes — is itself a substantive theological question. The book makes the case, against both academic-survey and confessional-summary modes, that the canonical texts of any classical religious tradition deserve to be read in the same disciplined way one would read Aquinas — slowly, with the *vyākhyāna*-tradition in hand, with willingness to be reshaped by the encounter.

engaged_works.title: Mimamsa as Introspective Literature and as Philosophy
engaged_works.title_iast: Mīmāṃsā as Introspective Literature and as Philosophy
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Published 2022, Springer Encyclopedia of Indian Religions, ed. Long et al., entry 1397; DOI 10.1007/978-94-024-1188-1_1397.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored encyclopedia article (Springer Encyclopedia of Indian Religions 2022).

ascription_evidence.description: Reference-work article in a major Springer encyclopedia.

engaged_works.summary: An encyclopedia article that extends the 1990 *Thinking Ritually* reading into a sharper thesis: Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā is not only philosophy of language and ritual exegesis but *introspective literature* — a literature that thematises the practitioner's interior orientation to the sacred, the inner disposition (*śraddhā*) prerequisite to ritual qualification, the agent's intentional structure in performing the rite, and the cognitive transformation enacted by *codanā*. Clooney works specifically through Kumārila's *Ślokavārttika* — including the *Codanā-Sūtra* section's defense of *svataḥ-prāmāṇya*, the *ātmavāda* on the self-luminous *ātman*, and the Bhāṭṭa analysis of how injunctive cognition operates on the agent — and through Śabara's foundational gloss on Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra 1.1.2 (*so 'rthaḥ puruṣaṃ niḥśreyasena saṃyunaktīti*), to show that the *artha* of *codanā* is from the start defined by what it does to the *puruṣa*, not by what it is in the world. The article matters because it places, in a major reference work, the academic precedent for reading Mīmāṃsā as having a substantive interior register — a reading on which the school's polemic against the Vedāntic priority of *jñāna-kāṇḍa* over *karma-kāṇḍa* is compatible with an interior dimension within the ritual operation itself.

engaged_works.title: Hindu and Catholic, Priest and Scholar: A Love Story
engaged_works.title_iast: Hindu and Catholic, Priest and Scholar: A Love Story

ascription_evidence.description: Author's memoir, widely reviewed (Reading Religion, National Catholic Reporter, New Books Network).

engaged_works.summary: A spiritual-and-intellectual memoir tracing Clooney's path from mid-twentieth-century American Catholicism through his Jesuit formation and the long study of Sanskrit and Tamil that became his scholarly life, organized around the conviction that the disciplined reading of another tradition's primary texts is itself a contemplative practice. The book is included here, despite being autobiographical rather than primarily scholarly, because it is the most extended first-person statement of the methodological commitments — long apprenticeship in the languages and *vyākhyāna*-traditions of the other tradition, willingness to be reshaped by what one reads, refusal to subordinate the read tradition to the home tradition's prior categories — that govern the rest of the corpus. The memoir makes the methodological wager of comparative theology biographically intelligible: the Vedāntic and Mīmāṃsā material Clooney has engaged for fifty years has, by his own account, shaped his Catholic theology rather than merely receiving it.
